content.text: Class components in React define lifecycle through a set of methods on the component class. Although function components with hooks are now the recommended approach, understanding class lifecycle methods remains important: they appear extensively in existing codebases, in error boundary implementations, and in libraries that still ship class components.
The lifecycle divides into four categories: mounting, updating, unmounting, and error handling. Two additional static methods —
The mounting phase runs once when a component is inserted into the DOM. Four methods can participate, though most components only need one or two of them.
is undefined inside the constructor body. Since React 2022, class property syntax (
) makes the constructor optional for state initialisation.
is called before every render (both mount and update). It is a static method — it has no access to
and must return either a partial state object or
. Prefer simpler patterns (memo values, effect-driven state) whenever possible.
must be a pure function of
, mutate external state, or produce side-effects. It may return JSX, arrays, strings, numbers,

fires after the component's output has been committed to the DOM. It is the correct place to start data fetches, subscribe to stores, or set up timers. State updates inside it trigger a second render before the browser paints (visible to the user as a single frame).
is called, or when the parent re-renders and passes new props. The update sequence runs the same methods as mount, plus two additional ones.
to bail out of the render.
provides a shallow-equality implementation automatically — equivalent to writing
with a shallow comparison of props and state.
runs after the final render but before React commits changes to the DOM. Its return value becomes the third argument (
. A common use is capturing scroll position before a list grows.
receives the previous props, previous state, and the snapshot from
calls inside it with a condition that compares current and previous props — otherwise you trigger an infinite update loop.
The unmounting phase has a single method. It is the mirror of
— the component is about to be discarded, so state updates have no effect and React will log a warning.
Class components can also define error-handling lifecycle methods. These are the
lifecycle capabilities that have no hook equivalent — error boundaries must be class components.
logs the error. Both receive the thrown value and an
Three lifecycle methods were renamed with an
prefix in React 16.3 because they are incompatible with concurrent features (React 18+). They still work with the prefix but should not be used in new code. A future major version will remove them.

The underlying problem is that these methods fire during the render phase, which may now be interrupted and replayed by the concurrent renderer. Side-effects or API calls inside them can execute multiple times or interleave with other components' renders.
behaves differently depending on where it is called:
Automatic batching groups all setState calls
when new state depends on current state — React may batch multiple calls and the object form will use a stale snapshot.
performs a shallow comparison of props and state to implement

automatically. It is safe only when all values in props and state are primitive or held as stable references. Mutating an array or object in place without replacing the reference causes the component to skip the re-render even though the data changed.

and immediately queues a re-render. Use it only when you are managing external mutable state that React cannot observe — such as a third-party observable or a canvas drawing library. Avoid it in all other cases.
in the constructor. Callback refs (a function as the ref prop) also work and allow more dynamic assignment:
render-prop pattern (multiple contexts). Function components can use as many
calls as needed, which is why they are preferred.
When migrating a class component to a function component, follow this checklist:
Keep as a class; wrap function components inside it
can help with mechanical conversions, but always review the output — it cannot infer correct dependency arrays for effects.
Class lifecycle methods are a coherent system that served React well before hooks. The methods that matter for new work are:
for side-effects, and the error boundary pair for resilience. The unsafe trio (formerly
) should be replaced with hooks when you encounter them.

For new code, function components with hooks cover all these capabilities except error boundaries. When you encounter class components in existing projects, the methods above define the complete lifecycle surface you will need to read and reason about.
